- Page URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PDF
- Page Title: PDF - Wikipedia

          - table [ref=e236]:

                  - code [ref=e250]: .pdf
              - row "Internet media type application/pdf,[1] application/x-pdf application/x-bzpdf application/x-gzpdf" [ref=e251]:
                - rowheader "Internet media type" [ref=e252]:
                  - link "Internet media type" [ref=e253] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Media_type
                - cell "application/pdf,[1] application/x-pdf application/x-bzpdf application/x-gzpdf" [ref=e254]:
                  - list [ref=e256]:

                      - code [ref=e262]: application/x-pdf

                      - code [ref=e266]: application/x-gzpdf
              - row "Type code PDF [1] (including a single trailing space)" [ref=e267]:
                - rowheader "Type code" [ref=e268]:
                  - link "Type code" [ref=e269] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Resource_fork#Types
                - cell "PDF [1] (including a single trailing space)" [ref=e270]:
                  - code [ref=e271]: PDF
                  - superscript [ref=e272]:

                      - /url: "#cite_note-rfc8118-1"
                  - text: (including a single trailing space)
              - row "Uniform Type Identifier (UTI) com.adobe.pdf" [ref=e274]:
                - rowheader "Uniform Type Identifier (UTI)" [ref=e275]:

                    - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Uniform_Type_Identifier
                - cell "com.adobe.pdf" [ref=e277]
              - row "Magic number %PDF" [ref=e278]:
                - rowheader "Magic number" [ref=e279]:
                  - link "Magic number" [ref=e280] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File_format#Magic_number
                - cell "%PDF" [ref=e281]:
                  - code [ref=e282]: "%PDF"
              - row "Developed by Adobe Inc. (1991–2008) ISO (2008–)" [ref=e283]:
                - rowheader "Developed by" [ref=e284]
                - cell "Adobe Inc. (1991–2008) ISO (2008–)" [ref=e285]:
                  - link "Adobe Inc." [ref=e286] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adobe_Inc.
                  - text: (1991–2008)
                  - link "ISO" [ref=e287] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO
                  - text: (2008–)
              - row "Initial release June 15, 1993; 32 years ago" [ref=e288]:
                - rowheader "Initial release" [ref=e289]
                - cell "June 15, 1993; 32 years ago" [ref=e290]
              - row "Latest release 2.0" [ref=e291]:
                - rowheader "Latest release" [ref=e292]:
                  - link "Latest release" [ref=e293] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Software_release_life_cycle
                - cell "2.0" [ref=e294]:
                  - generic [ref=e295]: "2.0"
              - row "Extended to PDF/A, PDF/E, PDF/UA, PDF/VT, PDF/X" [ref=e296]:
                - rowheader "Extended to" [ref=e297]
                - cell "PDF/A, PDF/E, PDF/UA, PDF/VT, PDF/X" [ref=e298]:
                  - link "PDF/A" [ref=e299] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PDF/A
                  - text: ","
                  - link "PDF/E" [ref=e300] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PDF/E
                  - text: ","
                  - link "PDF/UA" [ref=e301] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PDF/UA
                  - text: ","
                  - link "PDF/VT" [ref=e302] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PDF/VT
                  - text: ","
                  - link "PDF/X" [ref=e303] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PDF/X
              - row "Standard ISO 32000-2" [ref=e304]:
                - rowheader "Standard" [ref=e305]:
                  - link "Standard" [ref=e306] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_standard
                - cell "ISO 32000-2" [ref=e307]
              - row "Open format? Yes" [ref=e308]:
                - rowheader "Open format?" [ref=e309]:
                  - generic [ref=e310]:
                    - link "Open format" [ref=e311] [cursor=pointer]:
                      - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Open_file_format
                    - text: "?"
                - cell "Yes" [ref=e312]
              - row "Website iso.org/standard/75839.html" [ref=e313]:
                - rowheader "Website" [ref=e314]
                - cell "iso.org/standard/75839.html" [ref=e315]:
                  - link "iso.org/standard/75839.html" [ref=e317] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: https://iso.org/standard/75839.html
                    - text: iso
                    - text: .org
                    - text: /standard
                    - text: /75839
                    - text: .html
          - paragraph [ref=e318]:
            - text: Portable Document Format (PDF), standardized as ISO 32000, is a
            - link "file format" [ref=e319] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File_format
            - text: developed by
            - link "Adobe" [ref=e320] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adobe_Inc.
            - text: in 1993 used to present
            - link "documents" [ref=e321] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Document
            - text: ", including text formatting and images, in a manner independent of"
            - link "application software" [ref=e322] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Application_software
            - text: ","
            - link "hardware" [ref=e323] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computer_hardware
            - text: ", and"
            - link "operating systems" [ref=e324] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Operating_system
            - text: .
            - superscript [ref=e325]:
              - link "[2]" [ref=e326] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-pdf-ref-1.7-2"
            - superscript [ref=e327]:
              - link "[3]" [ref=e328] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-3"
            - text: Based on the
            - link "PostScript" [ref=e329] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PostScript
            - text: language, each PDF file encapsulates a complete description of a fixed-layout document, including the text,
            - link "fonts" [ref=e330] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Font
            - text: ","
            - link "vector graphics" [ref=e331] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vector_graphics
            - text: ","
            - link "raster images" [ref=e332] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Raster_images
            - text: and other information needed to display it.
          - paragraph [ref=e333]:
            - text: PDF has its roots in "The Camelot Project" initiated by Adobe co-founder
            - link "John Warnock" [ref=e334] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Warnock
            - text: in 1991.
            - superscript [ref=e335]:
              - link "[4]" [ref=e336] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-4"
            - text: PDF was standardized as ISO 32000 in 2008.
            - superscript [ref=e337]:
              - link "[5]" [ref=e338] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-5"
            - text: It is maintained by ISO TC 171 SC 2 WG8, of which the
            - link "PDF Association" [ref=e339] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PDF_Association
            - text: is the committee manager.
            - superscript [ref=e340]:
              - link "[6]" [ref=e341] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-6"
            - text: The last edition as ISO 32000-2:2020 was published in December 2020.
            - superscript [ref=e342]:
              - link "[7]" [ref=e343] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-7"
          - paragraph [ref=e344]:
            - text: PDF files may contain a variety of content besides flat text and graphics including logical structuring elements, interactive elements such as annotations and form-fields,
            - link "layers" [ref=e345] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Layers_(digital_image_editing)
            - text: ","
            - link "rich media" [ref=e346] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rich_media
            - text: (including video content), three-dimensional objects using
            - link "U3D" [ref=e347] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/U3D
            - text: or
            - link "PRC" [ref=e348] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PRC_(file_format)
            - text: ", and various other"
            - link "data formats" [ref=e349] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File_format
            - text: . The PDF specification also provides for encryption and
            - link "digital signatures" [ref=e350] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Digital_signature
            - text: ", file attachments, and"
            - link "metadata" [ref=e351] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metadata
            - text: to enable
            - link "workflows" [ref=e352] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Workflow
            - text: requiring these features.
          - generic [ref=e353]:
            - heading "History" [level=2] [ref=e354]
            - generic [ref=e355]:
              - text: "["
              - link "edit" [ref=e356] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=PDF&action=edit&section=1
              - text: "]"
          - note [ref=e357]:
            - text: "Main article:"
            - link "History of PDF" [ref=e358] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_PDF
          - paragraph [ref=e359]:
            - text: The development of PDF began in 1991 when
            - link "John Warnock" [ref=e360] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Warnock
            - text: wrote a paper for a project then code-named Camelot, in which he proposed the creation of a simplified version of PostScript called Interchange PostScript (IPS).
            - superscript [ref=e361]:
              - link "[8]" [ref=e362] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-Pfiffner_Page_137-8"
            - text: Unlike traditional PostScript, which was tightly focused on rendering
            - link "print jobs" [ref=e363] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Print_job
            - text: to output devices, IPS would be optimized for displaying pages to any screen and any platform.
            - superscript [ref=e364]:
              - link "[8]" [ref=e365] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-Pfiffner_Page_137-8"
          - paragraph [ref=e366]:
            - link "Adobe Systems" [ref=e367] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adobe_Systems
            - text: made the PDF specification available free of charge in 1993.
            - superscript [ref=e368]:
              - link "[9]" [ref=e369] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-9"
            - text: In the early years PDF was popular mainly in
            - link "desktop publishing" [ref=e370] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desktop_publishing
            - text: workflows, and competed with several other formats, including
            - link "DjVu" [ref=e371] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DjVu
            - text: ","
            - link "Envoy" [ref=e372] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Envoy_(WordPerfect)
            - text: ", Common Ground Digital Paper, Farallon Replica and even Adobe's own PostScript format."
          - paragraph [ref=e373]:
            - text: PDF was a
            - link "proprietary format" [ref=e374] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proprietary_format
            - text: controlled by Adobe until it was released as an
            - link "open standard" [ref=e375] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Open_standard
            - text: on July 1, 2008, and published by the
            - link "International Organization for Standardization" [ref=e376] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_Organization_for_Standardization
            - text: as ISO 32000-1:2008,
            - superscript [ref=e377]:
              - link "[10]" [ref=e378] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-iso-standard-10"
            - superscript [ref=e379]:
              - link "[11]" [ref=e380] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-11"
            - text: at which time control of the specification passed to an ISO Committee of volunteer industry experts. In 2008, Adobe published a Public Patent License to ISO 32000-1 granting
            - link "royalty-free" [ref=e381] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Royalty-free
            - text: rights for all patents owned by Adobe necessary to make, use, sell, and distribute PDF-compliant implementations.
            - superscript [ref=e382]:
              - link "[12]" [ref=e383] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-12"
          - paragraph [ref=e384]:
            - text: PDF 1.7, the sixth edition of the PDF specification that became ISO 32000-1, includes some proprietary technologies defined only by Adobe, such as
            - link "Adobe XML Forms Architecture" [ref=e385] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adobe_XML_Forms_Architecture
            - text: (XFA) and
            - link "JavaScript" [ref=e386] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JavaScript
            - text: extension for Acrobat, which are referenced by ISO 32000-1 as
            - link "normative" [ref=e387] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Normative
            - text: and indispensable for the full implementation of the ISO 32000-1 specification.
            - superscript [ref=e388]:
              - link "[13]" [ref=e389] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-13"
            - text: These proprietary technologies are not standardized, and their specification is published only on Adobe's website.
            - superscript [ref=e390]:
              - link "[14]" [ref=e391] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-iso-meeting-n603-14"
            - superscript [ref=e392]:
              - link "[15]" [ref=e393] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-15"
            - superscript [ref=e394]:
              - link "[16]" [ref=e395] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-rosenthol-adobe-2012-16"
            - text: Many of them are not supported by popular third-party implementations of PDF.
          - paragraph [ref=e396]:
            - text: ISO published version 2.0 of PDF, ISO 32000-2 in 2017, available for purchase, replacing the free specification provided by Adobe.
            - superscript [ref=e397]:
              - link "[17]" [ref=e398] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-nowfree-17"
            - text: In December 2020, the second edition of PDF 2.0, ISO 32000-2:2020, was published, with clarifications, corrections, and critical updates to normative references
            - superscript [ref=e399]:
              - link "[18]" [ref=e400] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-18"
            - text: (ISO 32000-2 does not include any proprietary technologies as normative references).
            - superscript [ref=e401]:
              - link "[19]" [ref=e402] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-:0-19"
            - text: In April 2023 the PDF Association made ISO 32000-2 available for download free of charge.
            - superscript [ref=e403]:
              - link "[17]" [ref=e404] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-nowfree-17"
          - generic [ref=e405]:
            - heading "Technical details" [level=2] [ref=e406]
            - generic [ref=e407]:
              - text: "["
              - link "edit" [ref=e408] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=PDF&action=edit&section=2
              - text: "]"
          - paragraph [ref=e409]:
            - text: A PDF file is often a combination of
            - link "vector graphics" [ref=e410] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vector_graphics
            - text: ", text, and"
            - link "bitmap graphics" [ref=e411] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bitmap_graphics
            - text: ". The basic types of content in a PDF are:"
          - list [ref=e412]:
            - listitem [ref=e413]:
              - text: Typeset text stored as content streams (i.e., not encoded in
              - link "plain text" [ref=e414] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plain_text
              - text: );
            - listitem [ref=e415]: Vector graphics for illustrations and designs that consist of shapes and lines;
            - listitem [ref=e416]: Raster graphics for photographs and other types of images; and
            - listitem [ref=e417]: Other multimedia objects.
          - paragraph [ref=e418]: In later PDF revisions, a PDF document can also support links (inside document or web page), forms, JavaScript (initially available as a plugin for Acrobat 3.0), or any other types of embedded contents that can be handled using plug-ins.
          - paragraph [ref=e419]: "PDF combines three technologies:"
          - list [ref=e420]:
            - listitem [ref=e421]: An equivalent subset of the PostScript page description programming language but in declarative form, for generating the layout and graphics.
            - listitem [ref=e422]:
              - text: A
              - link "font-embedding" [ref=e423] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Font_embedding
              - text: /replacement system to allow fonts to travel with the documents.
            - listitem [ref=e424]:
              - text: A structured storage system to bundle these elements and any associated content into a single file, with
              - link "data compression" [ref=e425] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Data_compression
              - text: where appropriate.
          - generic [ref=e426]:
            - heading "PostScript language" [level=3] [ref=e427]
            - generic [ref=e428]:
              - text: "["
              - link "edit" [ref=e429] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=PDF&action=edit&section=3
              - text: "]"
          - paragraph [ref=e430]:
            - link "PostScript" [ref=e431] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PostScript
            - text: is a
            - link "page description language" [ref=e432] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Page_description_language
            - text: run in an
            - link "interpreter" [ref=e433] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interpreter_(computing)
            - text: to generate an image.
            - superscript [ref=e434]:
              - link "[8]" [ref=e435] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-Pfiffner_Page_137-8"
            - text: It can handle graphics and has standard features of
            - link "programming languages" [ref=e436] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Programming_language
            - text: such as
            - link "branching" [ref=e437] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conditional_(computer_programming)
            - text: and
            - link "looping" [ref=e438] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Loop_(computing)
            - text: .
            - superscript [ref=e439]:
              - link "[8]" [ref=e440] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-Pfiffner_Page_137-8"
            - text: PDF is a subset of PostScript, simplified to remove such
            - link "control flow" [ref=e441] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Control_flow
            - text: features, while graphics commands remain.
            - superscript [ref=e442]:
              - link "[8]" [ref=e443] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-Pfiffner_Page_137-8"
          - paragraph [ref=e444]:
            - text: PostScript was originally designed for a drastically different
            - link "use case" [ref=e445] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Use_case
            - text: ": transmission of one-way linear print jobs in which the PostScript interpreter would collect a series of commands until it encountered the"
            - code [ref=e446]: showpage
            - text: command, then execute all the commands to render a page as a raster image to a printing device.
            - superscript [ref=e447]:
              - link "[20]" [ref=e448] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-Pfiffner_Page_139-20"
            - text: PostScript was not intended for long-term storage and real-time interactive rendering of
            - link "electronic documents" [ref=e449] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electronic_document
            - text: to
            - link "computer monitors" [ref=e450] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computer_monitor
            - text: ", so there was no need to support anything other than consecutive rendering of pages."
            - superscript [ref=e451]:
              - link "[20]" [ref=e452] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-Pfiffner_Page_139-20"
            - text: If there was an error in the final printed output, the user would correct it at the application level and send a new print job in the form of an entirely new PostScript file. Thus, any given page in a PostScript file could be accurately rendered only as the cumulative result of executing all preceding commands to draw all previous pages—any of which could affect subsequent pages—plus the commands to draw that particular page, and there was no easy way to bypass that process to skip around to different pages.
            - superscript [ref=e453]:
              - link "[20]" [ref=e454] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-Pfiffner_Page_139-20"
          - paragraph [ref=e455]:
            - text: Traditionally, to go from PostScript to PDF, a source PostScript file (that is, an executable program) is used as the basis for generating PostScript-like PDF code (see, e.g.,
            - link "Adobe Distiller" [ref=e456] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adobe_Distiller
            - text: ). This is done by applying standard
            - link "compiler" [ref=e457] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Compiler
            - text: techniques like
            - link "loop unrolling" [ref=e458] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Loop_unrolling
            - text: ","
            - link "inlining" [ref=e459] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inline_expansion
            - text: and removing unused branches, resulting in code that is purely declarative and static.
            - superscript [ref=e460]:
              - link "[20]" [ref=e461] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-Pfiffner_Page_139-20"
            - text: The result is then packaged into a
            - link "container format" [ref=e462] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Container_format
            - text: ", together with all necessary"
            - link "dependencies" [ref=e463] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dependency_(computer_science)
            - text: for correct rendering (external files, graphics, or fonts to which the document refers), and
            - link "compressed" [ref=e464] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Data_compression
            - text: . Modern applications write to printer drivers that directly generate PDF rather than going through PostScript first.
          - paragraph [ref=e465]: "As a document format, PDF has several advantages over PostScript:"
          - list [ref=e466]:
            - listitem [ref=e467]:
              - text: PDF contains only static
              - link "declarative" [ref=e468] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Declarative_programming
              - text: PostScript code that can be processed as data, and does not require a full program
              - link "interpreter" [ref=e469] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interpreter_(computing)
              - text: or
              - link "compiler" [ref=e470] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Compiler
              - text: .
              - superscript [ref=e471]:
                - link "[20]" [ref=e472] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#cite_note-Pfiffner_Page_139-20"
              - text: This avoids the complexity and security risks of an engine with such a higher complexity level.
            - listitem [ref=e473]:
              - text: Like
              - link "Display PostScript" [ref=e474] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Display_PostScript
              - text: ", PDF has supported"
              - link "transparent graphics" [ref=e475] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transparency_(graphic)
              - text: since version 1.4, while standard PostScript does not.
            - listitem [ref=e476]:
              - text: PDF enforces the rule that the code for any particular page cannot affect any other pages.
              - superscript [ref=e477]:
                - link "[20]" [ref=e478] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#cite_note-Pfiffner_Page_139-20"
              - text: That rule is strongly recommended for PostScript code too, but has to be implemented explicitly (see, e.g., the
              - link "Document Structuring Conventions" [ref=e479] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Document_Structuring_Conventions
              - text: ), as PostScript is a full programming language that allows for such greater flexibilities and is not limited to the concepts of pages and documents.
            - listitem [ref=e480]:
              - text: All data required for rendering is included within the file itself, improving portability.
              - superscript [ref=e481]:
                - link "[21]" [ref=e482] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#cite_note-21"
          - paragraph [ref=e483]: "Its disadvantages are:"
          - list [ref=e484]:
            - listitem [ref=e485]:
              - text: A loss of flexibility, and limitation to a single use case.
              - superscript [ref=e486]:
                - text: "["
                - link "citation needed" [ref=e488] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed
                - text: "]"
            - listitem [ref=e489]:
              - text: A (sometimes much) larger file size.
              - superscript [ref=e490]:
                - link "[22]" [ref=e491] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#cite_note-22"
          - paragraph [ref=e492]:
            - text: "PDF since v1.6 supports embedding of interactive 3D documents: 3D drawings can be embedded using"
            - link "U3D" [ref=e493] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/U3D
            - text: or
            - link "PRC" [ref=e494] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PRC_(file_format)
            - text: and various other data formats.
            - superscript [ref=e495]:
              - link "[23]" [ref=e496] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-3d#1-23"
            - superscript [ref=e497]:
              - link "[24]" [ref=e498] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-24"
            - superscript [ref=e499]:
              - link "[25]" [ref=e500] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-25"
          - generic [ref=e501]:
            - heading "File format" [level=2] [ref=e502]
            - generic [ref=e503]:
              - text: "["
              - link "edit" [ref=e504] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=PDF&action=edit&section=4
              - text: "]"
          - paragraph [ref=e505]:
            - text: A PDF file is organized using
            - link "ASCII" [ref=e506] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ASCII
            - text: characters, except for certain elements that may have binary content. The file starts with a header containing a
            - link "magic number" [ref=e507] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File_format#Magic_number
            - text: (as a readable string) and the version of the format, for example
            - code [ref=e508]: "%PDF-1.7"
            - text: . The format is a subset of a COS ("Carousel" Object Structure) format.
            - superscript [ref=e509]:
              - link "[26]" [ref=e510] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-26"
            - text: "A COS tree file consists primarily of objects, of which there are nine types:"
            - superscript [ref=e511]:
              - link "[19]" [ref=e512] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-:0-19"
          - list [ref=e513]:
            - listitem [ref=e514]:
              - link "Boolean" [ref=e515] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Boolean_data_type
              - text: values, representing true or false
            - listitem [ref=e516]:
              - link "Real numbers" [ref=e517] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Real_number
            - listitem [ref=e518]:
              - link "Integers" [ref=e519] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Integer
            - listitem [ref=e520]:
              - link "Strings" [ref=e521] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/String_(computer_science)
              - text: ", enclosed within parentheses ("
              - code [ref=e522]: (...)
              - text: ) or represented as hexadecimal within single angle brackets (
              - code [ref=e523]: <...>
              - text: ). Strings may contain 8-bit characters.
            - listitem [ref=e524]:
              - text: Names, starting with a forward slash (
              - code [ref=e525]: /
              - text: )
            - listitem [ref=e526]:
              - link "Arrays" [ref=e527] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Array_data_type
              - text: ", ordered collections of objects enclosed within square brackets ("
              - code [ref=e528]: "[...]"
              - text: )
            - listitem [ref=e529]:
              - link "Dictionaries" [ref=e530] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dictionary_(data_structure)
              - text: ", collections of objects indexed by names enclosed within double angle brackets ("
              - code [ref=e531]: <<...>>
              - text: )
            - listitem [ref=e532]:
              - link "Streams" [ref=e533] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stream_(computing)
              - text: ", usually containing large amounts of optionally compressed binary data, preceded by a dictionary and enclosed between the"
              - code [ref=e534]: stream
              - text: and
              - code [ref=e535]: endstream
              - text: keywords.
            - listitem [ref=e536]:
              - text: The
              - link "null" [ref=e537] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pointer_(computer_programming)
              - text: object
          - paragraph [ref=e538]:
            - text: Comments using 8-bit characters prefixed with the percent sign (
            - code [ref=e539]: "%"
            - text: ) may be inserted.
          - paragraph [ref=e540]:
            - text: Objects may be either direct (embedded in another object) or indirect. Indirect objects are numbered with an object number and a generation number and defined between the
            - code [ref=e541]: obj
            - text: and
            - code [ref=e542]: endobj
            - text: keywords if residing in the document root. Beginning with PDF version 1.5, indirect objects (except other streams) may also be located in special streams known as object streams (marked
            - code [ref=e543]: /Type /ObjStm
            - text: ). This technique enables non-stream objects to have standard stream filters applied to them, reduces the size of files that have large numbers of small indirect objects and is especially useful for Tagged PDF. Object streams do not support specifying an object's generation number (other than 0).
          - paragraph [ref=e544]:
            - text: An index table, also called the cross-reference table, is located near the end of the file and gives the byte offset of each indirect object from the start of the file.
            - superscript [ref=e545]:
              - link "[27]" [ref=e546] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-27"
            - text: This design allows for efficient
            - link "random access" [ref=e547] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Random_access
            - text: to the objects in the file, and also allows for small changes to be made without rewriting the entire file (incremental update). Before PDF version 1.5, the table would always be in a special ASCII format, be marked with the
            - code [ref=e548]: xref
            - text: keyword, and follow the main body composed of indirect objects. Version 1.5 introduced optional cross-reference streams, which have the form of a standard stream object, possibly with filters applied. Such a stream may be used instead of the ASCII cross-reference table and contains the offsets and other information in binary format. The format is flexible in that it allows for integer width specification (using the
            - code [ref=e549]: /W
            - text: array), so that for example, a document not exceeding 64
            - link "KiB" [ref=e550] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/KiB
            - text: in size may dedicate only 2 bytes for object offsets. To ensure backward compatibility, a hybrid-reference PDF file may include both traditional cross-reference tables and cross-reference streams, allowing older PDF processors to read the file while still taking advantage of the new features introduced in version 1.5.
            - superscript [ref=e551]:
              - link "[28]" [ref=e552] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-28"
          - paragraph [ref=e553]: At the end of a PDF file is a footer containing
          - list [ref=e554]:
            - listitem [ref=e555]:
              - text: The
              - code [ref=e556]: startxref
              - text: keyword followed by an offset to the start of the cross-reference table (starting with the
              - code [ref=e557]: xref
              - text: keyword) or the cross-reference stream object, followed by
            - listitem [ref=e558]:
              - text: The
              - code [ref=e559]: "%%EOF"
              - link "end-of-file" [ref=e560] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/End-of-file
              - text: marker.
          - paragraph [ref=e561]:
            - text: If a cross-reference stream is not being used, the footer is preceded by the
            - code [ref=e562]: trailer
            - text: "keyword followed by a dictionary containing information that would otherwise be contained in the cross-reference stream object's dictionary:"
          - list [ref=e563]:
            - listitem [ref=e564]:
              - text: A reference to the root object of the tree structure, also known as the catalog (
              - code [ref=e565]: /Root
              - text: )
            - listitem [ref=e566]:
              - text: The count of indirect objects in the cross-reference table (
              - code [ref=e567]: /Size
              - text: )
            - listitem [ref=e568]: Other optional information
          - paragraph [ref=e569]:
            - text: Within each page, there are one or multiple content streams that describe the text, vector and images being drawn on the page. The content stream is
            - link "stack-based" [ref=e570] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stack-oriented_programming_language
            - text: ", similar to PostScript."
            - superscript [ref=e571]:
              - link "[29]" [ref=e572] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-29"
          - figure "The maximum size of an Acrobat PDF page, superimposed on a map of Europe" [ref=e573]:
            - link [ref=e574] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Seitengroesse_PDF_7.png
              - img [ref=e575]
            - generic [ref=e576]: The maximum size of an Acrobat PDF page, superimposed on a map of Europe
          - paragraph [ref=e577]:
            - text: "There are two layouts to the PDF files: non-linearized (not \"optimized\") and linearized (\"optimized\"). Non-linearized PDF files can be smaller than their linear counterparts, though they are slower to access because portions of the data required to assemble pages of the document are scattered throughout the PDF file. Linearized PDF files (also called \"optimized\" or \"web optimized\" PDF files) are constructed in a manner that enables them to be read in a Web browser plugin without waiting for the entire file to download, since all objects required for the first page to display are optimally organized at the start of the file."
            - superscript [ref=e578]:
              - link "[30]" [ref=e579] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-pdf-ref-30"
            - text: PDF files may be optimized using
            - link "Adobe Acrobat" [ref=e580] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adobe_Acrobat
            - text: software or
            - link "QPDF" [ref=e581] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/QPDF
            - text: .
          - paragraph [ref=e582]:
            - text: Page dimensions are not limited by the format itself. However, Adobe Acrobat imposes a limit of 15 million by 15 million inches, or 225,000,000,000,000 square inches (145,161 km
            - superscript [ref=e583]: "2"
            - text: ; 56,047 sq mi), an area slightly larger than
            - link "Tajikistan" [ref=e584] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tajikistan
            - text: .
            - superscript [ref=e585]:
              - link "[2]" [ref=e586] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-pdf-ref-1.7-2"
            - superscript [ref=e587]: ": 1129"
          - generic [ref=e588]:
            - heading "Imaging model" [level=2] [ref=e589]
            - generic [ref=e590]:
              - text: "["
              - link "edit" [ref=e591] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=PDF&action=edit&section=5
              - text: "]"
          - paragraph [ref=e592]: The basic design of how graphics are represented in PDF is very similar to that of PostScript, except for the use of transparency, which was added in PDF 1.4.
          - paragraph [ref=e593]:
            - text: PDF graphics use a
            - link "device-independent" [ref=e594] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Device_independence
            - link "Cartesian coordinate system" [ref=e595] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cartesian_coordinate_system
            - text: to describe the surface of a page. A PDF page description can use a
            - link "matrix" [ref=e596] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Matrix_(mathematics)
            - text: to
            - link "scale" [ref=e597] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scale_(ratio)
            - text: ","
            - link "rotate" [ref=e598] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rotate
            - text: ", or"
            - link "skew" [ref=e599] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shear_mapping
            - text: "graphical elements. A key concept in PDF is that of the graphics state, which is a collection of graphical parameters that may be changed, saved, and restored by a page description. PDF has (as of version 2.0) 25 graphics state properties, of which some of the most important are:"
          - list [ref=e600]:
            - listitem [ref=e601]: The current transformation matrix (CTM), which determines the coordinate system
            - listitem [ref=e602]:
              - text: The
              - link "clipping path" [ref=e604] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Clipping_path
            - listitem [ref=e605]:
              - text: The
              - link "color space" [ref=e607] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Color_space
            - listitem [ref=e608]:
              - text: The
              - link "alpha constant" [ref=e610] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alpha_compositing
              - text: ", which is a key component of transparency"
            - listitem [ref=e611]:
              - link "Black point compensation" [ref=e613] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Black_point_compensation
              - text: control (introduced in PDF 2.0)
          - generic [ref=e614]:
            - heading "Vector graphics" [level=3] [ref=e615]
            - generic [ref=e616]:
              - text: "["
              - link "edit" [ref=e617] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=PDF&action=edit&section=6
              - text: "]"
          - paragraph [ref=e618]:
            - text: As in PostScript, vector graphics in PDF are constructed with paths. Paths are usually composed of lines and cubic
            - link "Bézier curves" [ref=e619] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/B%C3%A9zier_curve
            - text: ", but can also be constructed from the outlines of text. Unlike PostScript, PDF does not allow a single path to mix text outlines with lines and curves. Paths can be stroked, filled, fill then stroked, or used for"
            - link "clipping" [ref=e620] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Clipping_path
            - text: . Strokes and fills can use any color set in the graphics state, including patterns. PDF supports several types of patterns. The simplest is the tiling pattern in which a piece of artwork is specified to be drawn repeatedly. This may be a colored tiling pattern, with the colors specified in the pattern object, or an uncolored tiling pattern, which defers color specification to the time the pattern is drawn. Beginning with PDF 1.3 there is also a shading pattern, which draws continuously varying colors. There are seven types of shading patterns of which the simplest are the axial shading (Type 2) and radial shading (Type 3).
          - generic [ref=e621]:
            - heading "Raster images" [level=3] [ref=e622]
            - generic [ref=e623]:
              - text: "["
              - link "edit" [ref=e624] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=PDF&action=edit&section=7
              - text: "]"
          - paragraph [ref=e625]: "Raster images in PDF (called Image XObjects) are represented by dictionaries with an associated stream. The dictionary describes the properties of the image, and the stream contains the image data. (Less commonly, small raster images may be embedded directly in a page description as an inline image.) Images are typically filtered for compression purposes. Image filters supported in PDF include the following general-purpose filters:"
          - list [ref=e626]:
            - listitem [ref=e627]: ASCII85Decode, a filter used to put the stream into 7-bit ASCII,
            - listitem [ref=e628]: ASCIIHexDecode, similar to ASCII85Decode but less compact,
            - listitem [ref=e629]:
              - text: FlateDecode, a commonly used filter based on the
              - link "deflate" [ref=e630] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deflate
              - text: algorithm defined in
              - link "RFC" [ref=e631] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/RFC_(identifier)
              - link "1951" [ref=e632] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc1951
              - text: (deflate is also used in the
              - link "gzip" [ref=e633] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gzip
              - text: ","
              - link "PNG" [ref=e634] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portable_Network_Graphics
              - text: ", and"
              - link "zip" [ref=e635] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ZIP_(file_format)
              - text: "file formats among others); introduced in PDF 1.2; it can use one of two groups of predictor functions for more compact zlib/deflate compression: Predictor 2 from the"
              - link "TIFF" [ref=e636] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/TIFF
              - text: 6.0 specification and predictors (filters) from the
              - link "PNG" [ref=e637] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portable_Network_Graphics
              - text: specification (
              - link "RFC" [ref=e638] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/RFC_(identifier)
              - link "2083" [ref=e639] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc2083
              - text: ),
            - listitem [ref=e640]:
              - text: LZWDecode, a filter based on
              - link "LZW" [ref=e641] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/LZW
              - text: "Compression; it can use one of two groups of predictor functions for more compact LZW compression: Predictor 2 from the TIFF 6.0 specification and predictors (filters) from the PNG specification,"
            - listitem [ref=e642]:
              - text: RunLengthDecode, a simple compression method for streams with repetitive data using the
              - link "run-length encoding" [ref=e643] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Run-length_encoding
              - text: algorithm and the image-specific filters,
            - listitem [ref=e644]:
              - text: DCTDecode, a
              - link "lossy" [ref=e645] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lossy
              - text: filter based on the
              - link "JPEG" [ref=e646] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JPEG
              - text: standard,
            - listitem [ref=e647]:
              - text: CCITTFaxDecode, a lossless
              - link "bi-level" [ref=e648] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bi-level_image
              - text: (black/white) filter based on the Group 3 or
              - link "Group 4" [ref=e649] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Group_4_compression
              - link "CCITT" [ref=e650] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CCITT
              - text: (ITU-T)
              - link "fax" [ref=e651] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fax
              - text: compression standard defined in ITU-T
              - link "T.4" [ref=e652] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/T.4
              - text: and T.6,
            - listitem [ref=e653]:
              - text: JBIG2Decode, a lossy or
              - link "lossless" [ref=e654] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lossless
              - text: bi-level (black/white) filter based on the
              - link "JBIG2" [ref=e655] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JBIG2
              - text: standard, introduced in PDF 1.4, and
            - listitem [ref=e656]:
              - text: JPXDecode, a lossy or lossless filter based on the
              - link "JPEG 2000" [ref=e657] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JPEG_2000
              - text: standard, introduced in PDF 1.5.
          - paragraph [ref=e658]:
            - text: Normally all image content in a PDF is embedded in the file. But PDF allows image data to be stored in external files by the use of external streams or Alternate Images. Standardized subsets of PDF, including
            - link "PDF/A" [ref=e659] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PDF/A
            - text: and
            - link "PDF/X" [ref=e660] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PDF/X
            - text: ", prohibit these features."
          - generic [ref=e661]:
            - heading "Text" [level=3] [ref=e662]
            - generic [ref=e663]:
              - text: "["
              - link "edit" [ref=e664] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=PDF&action=edit&section=8
              - text: "]"
          - paragraph [ref=e665]: Text in PDF is represented by text elements in page content streams. A text element specifies that characters should be drawn at certain positions. The characters are specified using the encoding of a selected font resource.
          - paragraph [ref=e666]:
            - text: A font object in PDF is a description of a digital
            - link "typeface" [ref=e667] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Typeface
            - text: ". It may either describe the characteristics of a typeface, or it may include an embedded font file. The latter case is called an embedded font while the former is called an unembedded font. The font files that may be embedded are based on widely used standard digital font formats:"
            - link "Type 1" [ref=e668] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PostScript_fonts
            - text: (and its compressed variant CFF),
            - link "TrueType" [ref=e669] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/TrueType
            - text: ", and (beginning with PDF 1.6)"
            - link "OpenType" [ref=e670] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OpenType
            - text: . Additionally PDF supports the Type 3 variant in which the components of the font are described by PDF graphic operators.
          - paragraph [ref=e671]:
            - text: Fourteen typefaces, known as the standard 14 fonts or base fourteen fonts,
            - superscript [ref=e672]:
              - link "[31]" [ref=e673] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-31"
            - text: "have a special significance in PDF documents:"
          - list [ref=e674]:
            - listitem [ref=e675]:
              - link "Times" [ref=e676] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Times_Roman
              - text: (v3) (in regular, italic, bold, and bold italic)
            - listitem [ref=e677]:
              - link "Courier" [ref=e678] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Courier_(typeface)
              - text: (in regular, oblique, bold and bold oblique)
            - listitem [ref=e679]:
              - link "Helvetica" [ref=e680] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Helvetica
              - text: (v3) (in regular, oblique, bold and bold oblique)
            - listitem [ref=e681]:
              - link "Symbol" [ref=e682] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Symbol_(typeface)
            - listitem [ref=e683]:
              - link "Zapf Dingbats" [ref=e684] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zapf_Dingbats
          - paragraph [ref=e685]:
            - text: These fonts, or suitable substitute fonts with the same metrics, should be available in most PDF readers, but they are not guaranteed to be available in the reader, and may only display correctly if the system has them installed.
            - superscript [ref=e686]:
              - link "[32]" [ref=e687] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-aquarium-32"
            - text: Fonts may be substituted if they are not embedded in a PDF.
          - paragraph [ref=e688]:
            - text: Within text strings, characters are shown using character codes (integers) that map to glyphs in the current font using an encoding. There are several predefined encodings, including WinAnsi, MacRoman, and many encodings for East Asian languages and a font can have its own built-in encoding. (Although the WinAnsi and MacRoman encodings are derived from the historical properties of the
            - link "Windows" [ref=e689] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microsoft_Windows
            - text: and
            - link "Macintosh" [ref=e690] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Classic_Mac_OS
            - text: operating systems, fonts using these encodings work equally well on any platform.) PDF can specify a predefined encoding to use, the font's built-in encoding or provide a lookup table of differences to a predefined or built-in encoding (not recommended with TrueType fonts).
            - superscript [ref=e691]:
              - link "[2]" [ref=e692] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-pdf-ref-1.7-2"
            - text: The encoding mechanisms in PDF were designed for Type 1 fonts, and the rules for applying them to TrueType fonts are complex.
          - paragraph [ref=e693]: For large fonts or fonts with non-standard glyphs, the special encodings Identity-H (for horizontal writing) and Identity-V (for vertical) are used. With such fonts, it is necessary to provide a ToUnicode table if semantic information about the characters is to be preserved.
          - paragraph [ref=e694]:
            - text: A text document which is
            - link "scanned" [ref=e695] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Image_scanner
            - text: to PDF without the text being recognised by
            - link "optical character recognition" [ref=e696] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Optical_character_recognition
            - text: (OCR) is an image, with no fonts or text properties.
          - generic [ref=e697]:
            - heading "Transparency" [level=3] [ref=e698]
            - generic [ref=e699]:
              - text: "["
              - link "edit" [ref=e700] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=PDF&action=edit&section=9
              - text: "]"
          - paragraph [ref=e701]: The original imaging model of PDF was opaque, similar to PostScript, where each object drawn on the page completely replaced anything previously marked in the same location. In PDF 1.4 the imaging model was extended to allow transparency. When transparency is used, new objects interact with previously marked objects to produce blending effects. The addition of transparency to PDF was done by means of new extensions that were designed to be ignored in products written to PDF 1.3 and earlier specifications. As a result, files that use a small amount of transparency might be viewed acceptably by older viewers, but files making extensive use of transparency could be viewed incorrectly by an older viewer.
          - paragraph [ref=e702]:
            - text: The transparency extensions are based on the key concepts of transparency groups, blending modes, shape, and alpha. The model is closely aligned with the features of
            - link "Adobe Illustrator" [ref=e703] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adobe_Illustrator
            - text: version 9. The
            - link "blend modes" [ref=e704] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Blend_modes
            - text: were based on those used by
            - link "Adobe Photoshop" [ref=e705] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adobe_Photoshop
            - text: at the time. When the PDF 1.4 specification was published, the formulas for calculating blend modes were kept secret by Adobe. They have since been published.
            - superscript [ref=e706]:
              - link "[33]" [ref=e707] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-33"
          - paragraph [ref=e708]: The concept of a transparency group in PDF specification is independent of existing notions of "group" or "layer" in applications such as Adobe Illustrator. Those groupings reflect logical relationships among objects that are meaningful when editing those objects, but they are not part of the imaging model.
          - generic [ref=e709]:
            - heading "Additional features" [level=2] [ref=e710]
            - generic [ref=e711]:
              - text: "["
              - link "edit" [ref=e712] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=PDF&action=edit&section=10
              - text: "]"
          - generic [ref=e713]:
            - heading "Logical structure and accessibility" [level=3] [ref=e714]: Logical structure and accessibility
            - generic [ref=e715]:
              - text: "["
              - link "edit" [ref=e716] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=PDF&action=edit&section=11
              - text: "]"
          - note [ref=e717]:
            - text: "See also:"
            - link "PDF/A-1" [ref=e718] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PDF/A-1
            - text: and
            - link "PDF/UA" [ref=e719] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PDF/UA
          - paragraph [ref=e720]:
            - text: A tagged PDF (see clause 14.8 in ISO 32000) includes document structure and semantics information to enable reliable text extraction and
            - link "accessibility" [ref=e721] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Accessibility
            - text: .
            - superscript [ref=e722]:
              - link "[34]" [ref=e723] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-34"
            - text: Technically speaking, tagged PDF is a stylized use of the format that builds on the logical structure framework introduced in PDF 1.3. Tagged PDF defines a set of standard structure types and attributes that allow page content (text, graphics, and images) to be extracted and reused for other purposes.
            - superscript [ref=e724]:
              - link "[35]" [ref=e725] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-35"
          - paragraph [ref=e726]:
            - text: Tagged PDF is not required in situations where a PDF file is intended only for print. Since the feature is optional, and since the rules for tagged PDF were relatively vague in ISO 32000-1, support for tagged PDF among consuming devices, including
            - link "assistive technology" [ref=e727] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Assistive_technology
            - text: (AT), is uneven as of 2021.
            - superscript [ref=e728]:
              - link "[36]" [ref=e729] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-36"
            - text: ISO 32000-2, however, includes an improved discussion of tagged PDF which is anticipated to facilitate further adoption.
          - paragraph [ref=e730]:
            - text: An ISO-standardized subset of PDF specifically targeted at accessibility,
            - link "PDF/UA" [ref=e731] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PDF/UA
            - text: ", was first published in 2012."
          - generic [ref=e732]:
            - heading "Optional Content Groups (layers)" [level=3] [ref=e733]: Optional Content Groups (layers)
            - generic [ref=e734]:
              - text: "["
              - link "edit" [ref=e735] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=PDF&action=edit&section=12
              - text: "]"
          - paragraph [ref=e736]: With the introduction of PDF version 1.5 (2003) came the concept of Layers. Layers, more formally known as Optional Content Groups (OCGs), refer to sections of content in a PDF document that can be selectively viewed or hidden by document authors or viewers. This capability is useful in CAD drawings, layered artwork, maps, multi-language documents, etc.
          - paragraph [ref=e737]: Basically, it consists of an Optional Content Properties Dictionary added to the document root. This dictionary contains an array of Optional Content Groups (OCGs), each describing a set of information and each of which may be individually displayed or suppressed, plus a set of Optional Content Configuration Dictionaries, which give the status (Displayed or Suppressed) of the given OCGs.
          - generic [ref=e738]:
            - heading "Encryption and signatures" [level=3] [ref=e739]
            - generic [ref=e740]:
              - text: "["
              - link "edit" [ref=e741] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=PDF&action=edit&section=13
              - text: "]"
          - paragraph [ref=e742]:
            - text: A PDF file may be
            - link "encrypted" [ref=e743] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Encrypted
            - text: ", for security, in which case a password is needed to view or edit the contents. PDF 2.0 defines 256-bit"
            - link "AES encryption" [ref=e744] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AES_encryption
            - text: as the standard for PDF 2.0 files. The PDF Reference also defines ways that third parties can define their own encryption systems for PDF.
          - paragraph [ref=e745]: PDF files may be digitally signed, to provide secure authentication; complete details on implementing digital signatures in PDF are provided in ISO 32000-2.
          - paragraph [ref=e746]:
            - text: PDF files may also contain embedded
            - link "DRM" [ref=e747] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Digital_rights_management
            - text: restrictions that provide further controls that limit copying, editing, or printing. These restrictions depend on the reader software to obey them, so the security they provide is limited.
          - paragraph [ref=e748]:
            - text: "The standard security provided by PDF consists of two different methods and two different passwords: a user password, which encrypts the file and prevents opening, and an owner password, which specifies operations that should be restricted even when the document is decrypted, which can include modifying, printing, or copying text and graphics out of the document, or adding or modifying text notes and"
            - link "AcroForm" [ref=e749] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: "#Forms"
            - text: fields. The user password encrypts the file, while the owner password does not, instead relying on client software to respect these restrictions. An owner password can easily be removed by software, including some free online services.
            - superscript [ref=e750]:
              - link "[37]" [ref=e751] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-37"
            - text: Thus, the use restrictions that a document author places on a PDF document are not secure, and cannot be assured once the file is distributed; this warning is displayed when applying such restrictions using Adobe Acrobat software to create or edit PDF files.
          - paragraph [ref=e752]:
            - text: Even without removing the password, most freeware or open source PDF readers ignore the permission "protections" and allow the user to print or make copies of excerpts of the text as if the document were not limited by password protection.
            - superscript [ref=e753]:
              - link "[38]" [ref=e754] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-38"
            - superscript [ref=e755]:
              - link "[39]" [ref=e756] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-39"
            - superscript [ref=e757]:
              - link "[40]" [ref=e758] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-40"
          - paragraph [ref=e759]:
            - text: Beginning with PDF 1.5, Usage rights (UR) signatures are used to enable additional interactive features that are not available by default in a particular PDF viewer application. The signature is used to validate that the permissions have been granted by a
            - link "bona fide" [ref=e760] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bona_fide
            - text: "granting authority. For example, it can be used to allow a user:"
            - superscript [ref=e761]:
              - link "[41]" [ref=e762] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-iso32000-41"
          - list [ref=e763]:
            - listitem [ref=e764]: To save the PDF document along with a modified form or annotation data
            - listitem [ref=e765]: Import form data files in FDF, XFDF, and text (CSV/TSV) formats
            - listitem [ref=e766]: Export form data files in FDF and XFDF formats
            - listitem [ref=e767]: Submit form data
            - listitem [ref=e768]:
              - link "Instantiate" [ref=e769] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/instantiate
              - text: new pages from named page templates
            - listitem [ref=e770]:
              - text: Apply a
              - link "digital signature" [ref=e771] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Digital_signature
              - text: to existing digital signature form field
            - listitem [ref=e772]: Create, delete, modify, copy, import, and export annotations
          - paragraph [ref=e773]:
            - text: For example, Adobe Systems grants permissions to enable additional features in Adobe Reader, using
            - link "public-key cryptography" [ref=e774] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Public-key_cryptography
            - text: . Adobe Reader verifies that the signature uses a
            - link "certificate" [ref=e775] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Public_key_certificate
            - text: from an Adobe-authorized certificate authority. Any PDF application can use this same mechanism for its own purposes.
            - superscript [ref=e776]:
              - link "[41]" [ref=e777] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-iso32000-41"
          - paragraph [ref=e778]:
            - text: Under specific circumstances including non-
            - link "patched" [ref=e779] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Patch_(computing)
            - text: systems of the receiver, the information the receiver of a
            - link "digital signed" [ref=e780] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Digital_signature
            - text: document sees can be manipulated by the sender after the document has been signed by the signer.
            - superscript [ref=e781]:
              - link "[42]" [ref=e782] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-42"
          - paragraph [ref=e783]:
            - link "PAdES" [ref=e784] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PAdES
            - text: (PDF Advanced Electronic Signatures) is a set of restrictions and extensions to PDF and ISO 32000-1
            - superscript [ref=e785]:
              - link "[43]" [ref=e786] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-ISO_32000-1-43"
            - text: making it suitable for
            - link "advanced electronic signatures" [ref=e787] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Advanced_electronic_signature
            - text: . This is published by
            - link "ETSI" [ref=e788] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ETSI
            - text: as TS 102 778.
            - superscript [ref=e789]:
              - link "[44]" [ref=e790] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-ETSI_TS_102_778-1_V1.1.1-44"
          - generic [ref=e791]:
            - heading "File attachments" [level=3] [ref=e792]
            - generic [ref=e793]:
              - text: "["
              - link "edit" [ref=e794] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=PDF&action=edit&section=14
              - text: "]"
          - paragraph [ref=e795]:
            - text: PDF files can have file attachments which processors may access and open or save to a local filesystem.
            - superscript [ref=e796]:
              - link "[45]" [ref=e797] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-45"
          - generic [ref=e798]:
            - heading "Metadata" [level=3] [ref=e799]
            - generic [ref=e800]:
              - text: "["
              - link "edit" [ref=e801] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=PDF&action=edit&section=15
              - text: "]"
          - paragraph [ref=e802]:
            - text: PDF files can contain two types of metadata.
            - superscript [ref=e803]:
              - link "[2]" [ref=e804] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-pdf-ref-1.7-2"
            - text: The first is the Document Information Dictionary, a set of key/value fields such as author, title, subject, creation and update dates. This is optional and is referenced from an
            - code [ref=e805]: Info
            - text: key in the trailer of the file. A small set of fields is defined and can be extended with additional text values if required. This method is deprecated in PDF 2.0.
          - paragraph [ref=e806]:
            - text: In PDF 1.4, support was added for Metadata Streams, using the
            - link "Extensible Metadata Platform" [ref=e807] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Extensible_Metadata_Platform
            - text: (XMP) to add XML standards-based extensible metadata as used in other file formats. PDF 2.0 allows metadata to be attached to any object in the document, such as information about embedded illustrations, fonts, and images, as well as the whole document (attaching to the document catalog), using an extensible schema.
          - paragraph [ref=e808]:
            - text: PDF documents can also contain display settings, including the page display layout and zoom level in a Viewer Preferences object. Adobe Reader uses these settings to override the user's default settings when opening the document.
            - superscript [ref=e809]:
              - link "[46]" [ref=e810] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-46"
            - text: The free Adobe Reader cannot remove these settings.
          - generic [ref=e811]:
            - heading "Accessibility" [level=3] [ref=e812]
            - generic [ref=e813]:
              - text: "["
              - link "edit" [ref=e814] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=PDF&action=edit&section=16
              - text: "]"
          - paragraph [ref=e815]:
            - text: PDF files can be created specifically to be accessible to people with disabilities.
            - superscript [ref=e816]:
              - link "[47]" [ref=e817] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-47"
            - superscript [ref=e818]:
              - link "[48]" [ref=e819] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-48"
            - superscript [ref=e820]:
              - link "[49]" [ref=e821] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-49"
            - superscript [ref=e822]:
              - link "[50]" [ref=e823] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-50"
            - superscript [ref=e824]:
              - link "[51]" [ref=e825] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-51"
            - text: PDF file formats in use as of 2014 can include tags, text equivalents, captions, audio descriptions, and more. Some software can automatically produce
            - link "tagged PDFs" [ref=e826] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PDF/A
            - text: ", but this feature is not always enabled by default."
            - superscript [ref=e827]:
              - link "[52]" [ref=e828] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-52"
            - superscript [ref=e829]:
              - link "[53]" [ref=e830] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-53"
            - text: Leading
            - link "screen readers" [ref=e831] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Screen_reader
            - text: ", including"
            - link "JAWS" [ref=e832] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JAWS_(screen_reader)
            - text: ","
            - link "Window-Eyes" [ref=e833] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Window-Eyes
            - text: ", Hal, and"
            - link "Kurzweil 1000 and 3000" [ref=e834] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kurzweil_Educational_Systems
            - text: can read tagged PDFs.
            - superscript [ref=e835]:
              - link "[54]" [ref=e836] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-54"
            - superscript [ref=e837]:
              - link "[55]" [ref=e838] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-55"
            - text: Moreover, tagged PDFs can be re-flowed and magnified for readers with visual impairments. Adding tags to older PDFs and those that are generated from scanned documents can present some challenges.
          - paragraph [ref=e839]: One of the significant challenges with PDF accessibility is that PDF documents have three distinct views, which, depending on the document's creation, can be inconsistent with each other. The three views are (i) the physical view, (ii) the tags view, and (iii) the content view. The physical view is displayed and printed (what most people consider a PDF document). The tags view is what screen readers and other assistive technologies use to deliver high-quality navigation and reading experience to users with disabilities. The content view is based on the physical order of objects within the PDF's content stream and may be displayed by software that does not fully support the tags' view, such as the Reflow feature in Adobe's Reader.
          - paragraph [ref=e840]:
            - link "PDF/UA" [ref=e841] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PDF/UA
            - text: ", the International Standard for accessible PDF based on ISO 32000-1 was first published as ISO 14289–1 in 2012 and establishes normative language for accessible PDF technology. PDF/UA addresses accessibility of the PDF format it brings the ideas behind WCAG 2.0 to establish PDF-specific accessibility rules."
            - superscript [ref=e842]:
              - link "[56]" [ref=e843] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-56"
          - generic [ref=e844]:
            - heading "Multimedia" [level=3] [ref=e845]
            - generic [ref=e846]:
              - text: "["
              - link "edit" [ref=e847] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=PDF&action=edit&section=17
              - text: "]"
          - paragraph [ref=e848]: Rich Media PDF is a PDF file including interactive content that can be embedded or linked within the file. It can contain images, audio, video content, or buttons. For example, if the interactive PDF is a digital catalog for an E-commerce business, products can be listed on the PDF pages and can be added with images and links to the website and buttons to order directly from the document.
          - generic [ref=e849]:
            - heading "Forms" [level=3] [ref=e850]
            - generic [ref=e851]:
              - text: "["
              - link "edit" [ref=e852] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=PDF&action=edit&section=18
              - text: "]"
          - paragraph [ref=e853]:
            - text: "Interactive Forms is a mechanism to add forms to the PDF file format. PDF currently supports two different methods for integrating data and PDF forms. Both formats today coexist in the PDF specification:"
            - superscript [ref=e854]:
              - link "[41]" [ref=e855] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-iso32000-41"
            - superscript [ref=e856]:
              - link "[57]" [ref=e857] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-57"
            - superscript [ref=e858]:
              - link "[58]" [ref=e859] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-58"
            - superscript [ref=e860]:
              - link "[59]" [ref=e861] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-59"
          - list [ref=e862]:
            - listitem [ref=e863]: AcroForms (also known as Acrobat forms), introduced in the PDF 1.2 format specification and included in all later PDF specifications.
            - listitem [ref=e864]:
