              - link "XML Forms Architecture" [ref=e865] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/XML_Forms_Architecture
              - text: (XFA) forms, introduced in the PDF 1.5 format specification. Adobe XFA Forms are not compatible with AcroForms.
              - superscript [ref=e866]:
                - link "[60]" [ref=e867] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#cite_note-60"
              - text: XFA was deprecated from PDF with PDF 2.0.
          - paragraph [ref=e868]:
            - text: AcroForms were introduced in the PDF 1.2 format. AcroForms permit the uses of objects (e.g.
            - link "text boxes" [ref=e869] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Text_box
            - text: ","
            - link "Radio buttons" [ref=e870] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radio_button
            - text: ", etc.) and some code (e.g. JavaScript). Alongside the standard PDF action types, interactive forms (AcroForms) support submitting, resetting, and importing data. The \"submit\" action transmits the names and values of selected interactive form fields to a specified uniform resource locator (URL). Interactive form field names and values may be submitted in any of the following formats, (depending on the settings of the action's ExportFormat, SubmitPDF, and XFDF flags):"
            - superscript [ref=e871]:
              - link "[41]" [ref=e872] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-iso32000-41"
          - generic [ref=e873]:
            - term [ref=e874]: HTML Form format
            - definition [ref=e875]: HTML 4.01 Specification since PDF 1.5; HTML 2.0 since 1.2
            - term [ref=e876]: Forms Data Format (FDF)
            - definition [ref=e877]:
              - text: based on PDF, uses the same syntax and has essentially the same file structure, but is much simpler than PDF since the body of an FDF document consists of only one required object. Forms Data Format is defined in the PDF specification (since PDF 1.2). The Forms Data Format can be used when submitting form data to a server, receiving the response, and incorporating it into the interactive form. It can also be used to export form data to stand-alone files that can be imported back into the corresponding PDF interactive form. FDF was originally defined in 1996 as part of ISO 32000-2:2017.
              - superscript [ref=e878]:
                - text: "["
                - link "citation needed" [ref=e880] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed
                - text: "]"
            - term [ref=e881]: XML Forms Data Format (XFDF)
            - definition [ref=e882]:
              - text: (external XML Forms Data Format Specification, Version 2.0; supported since PDF 1.5; it replaced the "XML" form submission format defined in PDF 1.4) the XML version of Forms Data Format, but the XFDF implements only a subset of FDF containing forms and annotations. Some entries in the FDF dictionary do not have XFDF equivalents – such as the Status, Encoding, JavaScript, Page's keys, EmbeddedFDFs, Differences, and Target. In addition, XFDF does not allow the spawning, or addition, of new pages based on the given data; as can be done when using an FDF file. The XFDF specification is referenced (but not included) in PDF 1.5 specification (and in later versions). It is described separately in XML Forms Data Format Specification.
              - superscript [ref=e883]:
                - link "[61]" [ref=e884] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#cite_note-xfdf-61"
              - text: "The PDF 1.4 specification allowed form submissions in XML format, but this was replaced by submissions in XFDF format in the PDF 1.5 specification. XFDF conforms to the XML standard. XFDF can be used in the same way as FDF; e.g., form data is submitted to a server, modifications are made, then sent back and the new form data is imported in an interactive form. It can also be used to export form data to stand-alone files that can be imported back into the corresponding PDF interactive form. As of August 2019, XFDF 3.0 is an ISO/IEC standard under the formal name ISO 19444-1:2019 - Document management — XML Forms Data Format — Part 1: Use of ISO 32000-2 (XFDF 3.0)."
              - superscript [ref=e885]:
                - link "[62]" [ref=e886] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#cite_note-62"
              - text: This standard is a normative reference of ISO 32000-2.
            - term [ref=e887]: PDF
          - paragraph [ref=e888]: The entire document can be submitted rather than individual fields and values, as was defined in PDF 1.4.
          - paragraph [ref=e889]:
            - text: AcroForms can keep form field values in external stand-alone files containing key-value pairs. The external files may use Forms Data Format (FDF) and XML Forms Data Format (XFDF) files.
            - superscript [ref=e890]:
              - link "[63]" [ref=e891] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-63"
            - superscript [ref=e892]:
              - link "[61]" [ref=e893] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-xfdf-61"
            - superscript [ref=e894]:
              - link "[64]" [ref=e895] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-fdf-exchange-64"
            - text: The usage rights (UR) signatures define rights for import form data files in FDF, XFDF, and text (
            - link "CSV" [ref=e896] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Comma-separated_values
            - text: /
            - link "TSV" [ref=e897] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Delimiter-separated_values
            - text: ) formats, and export form data files in FDF and XFDF formats.
            - superscript [ref=e898]:
              - link "[41]" [ref=e899] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-iso32000-41"
          - paragraph [ref=e900]:
            - text: In PDF 1.5, Adobe Systems introduced a proprietary format for forms;
            - link "Adobe XML Forms Architecture" [ref=e901] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adobe_XML_Forms_Architecture
            - text: (XFA). Adobe XFA Forms are not compatible with ISO 32000's AcroForms feature, and most PDF processors do not handle XFA content. The XFA specification is referenced from ISO 32000-1/PDF 1.7 as an external proprietary specification and was entirely deprecated from PDF with ISO 32000-2 (PDF 2.0).
          - generic [ref=e902]:
            - heading "Licensing" [level=2] [ref=e903]
            - generic [ref=e904]:
              - text: "["
              - link "edit" [ref=e905] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=PDF&action=edit&section=19
              - text: "]"
          - paragraph [ref=e906]:
            - text: Anyone may create applications that can read and write PDF files without having to pay royalties to Adobe Systems. Adobe holds patents to PDF, but licenses them for royalty-free use in developing software complying with its PDF specification.
            - superscript [ref=e907]:
              - link "[65]" [ref=e908] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-65"
          - generic [ref=e909]:
            - heading "Security" [level=2] [ref=e910]
            - generic [ref=e911]:
              - text: "["
              - link "edit" [ref=e912] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=PDF&action=edit&section=20
              - text: "]"
          - note [ref=e913]:
            - text: "See also:"
            - link "Adobe Acrobat § Security" [ref=e914] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adobe_Acrobat#Security
          - generic [ref=e915]:
            - heading "Changes to content" [level=3] [ref=e916]
            - generic [ref=e917]:
              - text: "["
              - link "edit" [ref=e918] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=PDF&action=edit&section=21
              - text: "]"
          - paragraph [ref=e919]:
            - text: In November 2019, researchers from
            - link "Ruhr University Bochum" [ref=e920] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ruhr_University_Bochum
            - text: and Hackmanit GmbH published attacks on digitally signed PDFs.
            - superscript [ref=e921]:
              - link "[66]" [ref=e922] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-66"
            - text: They showed how to change the visible content in a signed PDF without invalidating the signature in 21 of 22 desktop PDF viewers and 6 of 8 online validation services by abusing implementation flaws. At the same conference, they additionally showed how to exfiltrate the plaintext of encrypted content in PDFs.
            - superscript [ref=e923]:
              - link "[67]" [ref=e924] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-67"
            - text: In 2021, they showed new so-called shadow attacks on PDFs that abuse the flexibility of features provided in the specification.
            - superscript [ref=e925]:
              - link "[68]" [ref=e926] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-68"
            - text: An overview of security issues in PDFs regarding
            - link "denial of service" [ref=e927] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Denial_of_service
            - text: ","
            - link "information disclosure" [ref=e928] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Data_breach
            - text: ","
            - link "data manipulation" [ref=e929] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Data_manipulation
            - text: ", and"
            - link "arbitrary code execution" [ref=e930] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arbitrary_code_execution
            - text: attacks was presented by Jens Müller.
            - superscript [ref=e931]:
              - link "[69]" [ref=e932] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-69"
            - superscript [ref=e933]:
              - link "[70]" [ref=e934] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-70"
          - generic [ref=e935]:
            - heading "Malware vulnerability" [level=3] [ref=e936]
            - generic [ref=e937]:
              - text: "["
              - link "edit" [ref=e938] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=PDF&action=edit&section=22
              - text: "]"
          - paragraph [ref=e939]:
            - text: Some popular PDF readers have a history of security vulnerabilities that allows PDF files that have been infected with viruses, Trojans, and other malware to inflict damage. Such PDF files can have hidden JavaScript code that might exploit vulnerabilities in a PDF reader, hidden objects executed when the file that hides them is opened, and, less commonly, a malicious PDF can launch malware.
            - superscript [ref=e940]:
              - link "[71]" [ref=e941] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-71"
          - paragraph [ref=e942]:
            - text: PDF attachments carrying viruses were first discovered in 2001. The virus, named OUTLOOK.PDFWorm or Peachy, uses
            - link "Microsoft Outlook" [ref=e943] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microsoft_Outlook
            - text: to send itself as an attached Adobe PDF file. It was activated with Adobe Acrobat, but not with Acrobat Reader.
            - superscript [ref=e944]:
              - link "[72]" [ref=e945] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-72"
          - paragraph [ref=e946]:
            - text: Over the years, several vulnerabilities have been discovered in various versions of Adobe Reader,
            - superscript [ref=e947]:
              - link "[73]" [ref=e948] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-73"
            - text: which prompted the company to issue security fixes. Vulnerabilities have been discovered in other PDF readers as well. One aggravating factor is that a PDF reader can be configured to start automatically if a web page has an embedded PDF file, providing a vector for attack. If a malicious web page contains an infected PDF file that takes advantage of a vulnerability in the PDF reader, the system may be compromised even if the browser is secure. Some of these vulnerabilities are a result of badly written PDF readers mishandling JavaScript embedded in the PDF file. Disabling JavaScript execution in the PDF reader can help mitigate such future exploits, although it does not protect against exploits in other parts of the PDF viewing software. Some security experts say that JavaScript is not essential for a PDF reader and that the security benefit that comes from disabling JavaScript outweighs any compatibility issues caused.
            - superscript [ref=e949]:
              - link "[74]" [ref=e950] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-74"
            - text: One way of avoiding PDF file exploits is to have a local or web service convert files to another format before viewing.
          - paragraph [ref=e951]:
            - text: On March 30, 2010, security researcher Didier Stevens reported an Adobe Reader and Foxit Reader exploit that runs a malicious executable if the user allows it to launch when asked.
            - superscript [ref=e952]:
              - link "[75]" [ref=e953] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-75"
          - generic [ref=e954]:
            - heading "Zip bomb" [level=3] [ref=e955]
            - generic [ref=e956]:
              - text: "["
              - link "edit" [ref=e957] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=PDF&action=edit&section=23
              - text: "]"
          - note [ref=e958]:
            - text: "See also:"
            - link "Zip bomb" [ref=e959] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zip_bomb
          - paragraph [ref=e960]:
            - text: PDF streams can have nested filters, which allows one to craft a 5 kilobyte file that unpacks to 1 petabyte in RAM. This can be used to cause a
            - link "denial of service" [ref=e961] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Denial_of_service
            - text: with implementations that don't guard against this like it was the case with
            - link "pypdf" [ref=e962] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: https://github.com/py-pdf/pypdf
            - text: "'s CVE-2025-55197."
            - superscript [ref=e963]:
              - link "[76]" [ref=e964] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-76"
            - superscript [ref=e965]:
              - link "[77]" [ref=e966] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-77"
          - generic [ref=e967]:
            - heading "Software" [level=2] [ref=e968]
            - generic [ref=e969]:
              - text: "["
              - link "edit" [ref=e970] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=PDF&action=edit&section=24
              - text: "]"
          - note [ref=e971]:
            - text: For a more comprehensive list, see
            - link "List of PDF software" [ref=e972] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_PDF_software
            - text: .
          - generic [ref=e973]:
            - heading "Viewers and editors" [level=3] [ref=e974]
            - generic [ref=e975]:
              - text: "["
              - link "edit" [ref=e976] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=PDF&action=edit&section=25
              - text: "]"
          - paragraph [ref=e977]:
            - text: Many PDF viewers are provided free of charge from a variety of sources. Programs to manipulate and edit PDF files are available, usually for purchase. Additionally, most modern web browsers, including
            - link "Chrome" [ref=e978] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Google_Chrome
            - text: ","
            - link "Firefox" [ref=e979] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mozilla_Firefox
            - text: ", and"
            - link "Safari" [ref=e980] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Safari_(web_browser)
            - text: ", include PDF viewing capabilities, replacing"
            - link "browser plugins" [ref=e981] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Browser_plugin
            - text: that were previously created for such purposes.
            - superscript [ref=e982]:
              - link "[78]" [ref=e983] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-78"
          - paragraph [ref=e984]:
            - text: There are many software options for creating PDFs, including the PDF printing capabilities built into
            - link "macOS" [ref=e985] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MacOS
            - text: ","
            - link "iOS" [ref=e986] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IOS
            - text: ","
            - superscript [ref=e987]:
              - link "[79]" [ref=e988] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-79"
            - text: and most
            - link "Linux" [ref=e989] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Linux
            - text: distributions. Much document processing software including
            - link "LibreOffice" [ref=e990] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/LibreOffice
            - text: ","
            - link "Microsoft Office 2007" [ref=e991] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microsoft_Office_2007
            - text: (if updated to
            - link "SP2" [ref=e992] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Office_2007#Service_Pack_2
            - text: ) and later,
            - superscript [ref=e993]:
              - link "[80]" [ref=e994] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-80"
            - link "WordPerfect" [ref=e995] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/WordPerfect
            - text: 9, and
            - link "Scribus" [ref=e996] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scribus
            - text: can export documents in PDF. There are many PDF print drivers for Microsoft Windows, the
            - link "pdfTeX" [ref=e997] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PdfTeX
            - text: typesetting system, the
            - link "DocBook" [ref=e998] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DocBook
            - text: PDF tools, applications developed around
            - link "Ghostscript" [ref=e999] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ghostscript
            - text: and
            - link "Adobe Acrobat" [ref=e1000] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adobe_Acrobat
            - text: itself as well as
            - link "Adobe InDesign" [ref=e1001] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adobe_InDesign
            - text: ","
            - link "Adobe FrameMaker" [ref=e1002] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adobe_FrameMaker
            - text: ", Adobe Illustrator, Adobe Photoshop, that allow a \"PDF printer\" to be set up, which when selected sends output to a PDF file instead of a physical printer."
            - link "Google" [ref=e1003] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Google
            - text: "'s online office suite"
            - link "Google Docs" [ref=e1004] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Google_Docs
            - text: allows uploading and saving to PDF. Some web apps offer free PDF editing and annotation tools.
          - paragraph [ref=e1005]:
            - text: The
            - link "Free Software Foundation" [ref=e1006] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Free_Software_Foundation
            - text: was "developing a free, high-quality and fully functional set of libraries and programs that implement the PDF file format and associated technologies to the ISO 32000 standard", as one of its
            - link "high priority projects" [ref=e1007] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High_priority_free_software_projects
            - text: .
            - superscript [ref=e1008]:
              - link "[81]" [ref=e1009] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-81"
            - superscript [ref=e1010]:
              - link "[82]" [ref=e1011] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-82"
            - text: In 2011, however, the GNU PDF project was removed from the list of "high priority projects" due to the maturation of the
            - link "Poppler library" [ref=e1012] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Poppler_(software)
            - text: ","
            - superscript [ref=e1013]:
              - link "[83]" [ref=e1014] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-83"
            - text: which has enjoyed wider use in applications such as
            - link "Evince" [ref=e1015] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evince
            - text: with the
            - link "GNOME" [ref=e1016] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GNOME
            - text: desktop environment. Poppler is based on the
            - link "Xpdf" [ref=e1017] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Xpdf
            - superscript [ref=e1018]:
              - link "[84]" [ref=e1019] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-84"
            - superscript [ref=e1020]:
              - link "[85]" [ref=e1021] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-85"
            - text: code base. There are also commercial development libraries available as listed in
            - link "List of PDF software" [ref=e1022] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_PDF_software
            - text: .
          - paragraph [ref=e1023]:
            - text: The
            - link "Apache PDFBox" [ref=e1024] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apache_PDFBox
            - text: project of the
            - link "Apache Software Foundation" [ref=e1025] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apache_Software_Foundation
            - text: is an open source Java library, licensed under the
            - link "Apache License" [ref=e1026] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apache_License
            - text: ", for working with PDF documents."
            - superscript [ref=e1027]:
              - link "[86]" [ref=e1028] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-86"
          - generic [ref=e1029]:
            - heading "Printing" [level=3] [ref=e1030]
            - generic [ref=e1031]:
              - text: "["
              - link "edit" [ref=e1032] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=PDF&action=edit&section=26
              - text: "]"
          - paragraph [ref=e1033]:
            - link "Raster image processors" [ref=e1034] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Raster_image_processor
            - text: (RIPs) are used to convert PDF files into a
            - link "raster format" [ref=e1035] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Raster_graphics
            - text: suitable for imaging onto paper and other media in printers, digital production presses and
            - link "prepress" [ref=e1036] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prepress
            - text: in a process known as
            - link "rasterization" [ref=e1037] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rasterization
            - text: . RIPs capable of processing PDF directly include the Adobe PDF Print Engine
            - superscript [ref=e1038]:
              - link "[87]" [ref=e1039] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-87"
            - text: from Adobe Systems and Jaws
            - superscript [ref=e1040]:
              - link "[88]" [ref=e1041] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-88"
            - text: and the Harlequin RIP from
            - link "Global Graphics" [ref=e1042] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Global_Graphics
            - text: .
          - paragraph [ref=e1043]:
            - text: In 1993, the Jaws raster image processor from Global Graphics became the first shipping prepress RIP that interpreted PDF natively without conversion to another format. The company released an upgrade to its Harlequin RIP with the same capability in 1997.
            - superscript [ref=e1044]:
              - link "[89]" [ref=e1045] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-89"
          - paragraph [ref=e1046]:
            - link "Agfa-Gevaert" [ref=e1047] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Agfa-Gevaert
            - text: introduced and shipped Apogee, the first prepress workflow system based on PDF, in 1997.
          - paragraph [ref=e1048]:
            - text: Many commercial offset printers have accepted the submission of press-ready PDF files as a print source, specifically the PDF/X-1a subset and variations of the same.
            - superscript [ref=e1049]:
              - link "[90]" [ref=e1050] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-90"
            - text: The submission of press-ready PDF files is a replacement for the problematic need for receiving collected native working files.
          - paragraph [ref=e1051]:
            - text: In 2006, PDF was widely accepted as the standard print job format at the
            - link "Open Source Development Labs" [ref=e1052] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Open_Source_Development_Labs
            - text: Printing Summit. It is supported as a print job format by the
            - link "Common Unix Printing System" [ref=e1053] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CUPS
            - text: and desktop application projects such as GNOME,
            - link "KDE" [ref=e1054] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/KDE
            - text: ","
            - link "Firefox" [ref=e1055] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Firefox
            - text: ","
            - link "Thunderbird" [ref=e1056] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mozilla_Thunderbird
            - text: ", LibreOffice and"
            - link "OpenOffice" [ref=e1057] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OpenOffice.org
            - text: have switched to emit print jobs in PDF.
            - superscript [ref=e1058]:
              - link "[91]" [ref=e1059] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-91"
          - paragraph [ref=e1060]: Some desktop printers also support direct PDF printing, which can interpret PDF data without external help.
          - generic [ref=e1061]:
            - heading "Native display model" [level=3] [ref=e1062]
            - generic [ref=e1063]:
              - text: "["
              - link "edit" [ref=e1064] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=PDF&action=edit&section=27
              - text: "]"
          - link "icon" [ref=e1067] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg
            - img "icon" [ref=e1068]
          - generic [ref=e1069]:
            - text: This section
            - generic [ref=e1070]:
              - text: needs additional citations for
              - link "verification" [ref=e1071] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability
            - text: .
            - generic [ref=e1072]:
              - text: Please help
              - link "improve this article" [ref=e1073] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Special:EditPage/PDF
              - text: by
              - link "adding citations to reliable sources" [ref=e1074] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners
              - text: in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed.
            - generic [ref=e1076]: (July 2025)
            - generic [ref=e1078]:
              - text: (
              - link "Learn how and when to remove this message" [ref=e1080] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal
              - text: )
          - paragraph [ref=e1081]:
            - text: PDF was selected as the "native"
            - link "metafile" [ref=e1082] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metafile
            - text: format for
            - link "macOS" [ref=e1083] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MacOS
            - text: (originally called Mac OS X), replacing the
            - link "PICT" [ref=e1084] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PICT
            - text: format of the earlier
            - link "classic Mac OS" [ref=e1085] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Classic_Mac_OS
            - text: . The imaging model of the
            - link "Quartz" [ref=e1086] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quartz_(graphics_layer)
            - text: graphics layer is based on the model common to
            - link "Display PostScript" [ref=e1087] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Display_PostScript
            - text: and PDF, leading to the nickname Display PDF. The
            - link "Preview" [ref=e1088] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Preview_(macOS)
            - text: application can display PDF files, as can version 2.0 and later of the
            - link "Safari" [ref=e1089] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Safari_(web_browser)
            - text: web browser.
            - superscript [ref=e1090]:
              - link "[92]" [ref=e1091] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-92"
            - superscript [ref=e1092]:
              - link "[93]" [ref=e1093] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-93"
            - text: System-level support for PDF allows macOS applications to create PDF documents automatically, provided they support the OS-standard printing architecture. The files are then exported in PDF 1.3 format according to the file header. When taking a screenshot under Mac OS X versions 10.0 through 10.3, the image was also captured as a PDF; later versions save screen captures as a PNG file, though this behavior can be set back to PDF if desired.
          - generic [ref=e1094]:
            - heading "Annotation" [level=3] [ref=e1095]
            - generic [ref=e1096]:
              - text: "["
              - link "edit" [ref=e1097] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=PDF&action=edit&section=28
              - text: "]"
          - link "icon" [ref=e1100] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg
            - img "icon" [ref=e1101]
          - generic [ref=e1102]:
            - text: This section
            - generic [ref=e1103]:
              - text: does not
              - link "cite" [ref=e1104] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources
              - text: any
              - link "sources" [ref=e1105] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability
            - text: .
            - generic [ref=e1106]:
              - text: Please help
              - link "improve this section" [ref=e1107] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Special:EditPage/PDF
              - text: by
              - link "adding citations to reliable sources" [ref=e1108] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners
              - text: . Unsourced material may be challenged and
              - link "removed" [ref=e1109] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence
              - text: .
            - generic [ref=e1111]: (November 2023)
            - generic [ref=e1113]:
              - text: (
              - link "Learn how and when to remove this message" [ref=e1115] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal
              - text: )
          - note [ref=e1116]:
            - text: "See also:"
            - link "Comparison of note-taking software" [ref=e1117] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Comparison_of_note-taking_software
          - paragraph [ref=e1118]:
            - text: Adobe Acrobat is one example of proprietary software that allows the user to annotate, highlight, and add notes to already created PDF files. One UNIX application available as
            - link "free software" [ref=e1119] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Free_software
            - text: (under the
            - link "GNU General Public License" [ref=e1120] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GNU_General_Public_License
            - text: ) is
            - link "PDFedit" [ref=e1121] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PDFedit
            - text: . The freeware
            - link "Foxit Reader" [ref=e1122] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Foxit_Reader
            - text: ", available for Microsoft Windows, macOS and Linux, allows annotating documents. Tracker Software's"
            - link "PDF-XChange Viewer" [ref=e1123] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PDF-XChange_Viewer
            - text: allows annotations and markups without restrictions in its
            - link "freeware" [ref=e1124] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Freeware
            - text: alternative.
            - link "Apple" [ref=e1125] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apple_Inc.
            - text: "'s macOS's integrated PDF viewer, Preview, does also enable annotations as does the open-source software"
            - link "Skim" [ref=e1126] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Skim_(software)
            - text: ", with the latter supporting interaction with"
            - link "LaTeX" [ref=e1127] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/LaTeX
            - text: ", SyncTeX, and PDFSync and integration with"
            - link "BibDesk" [ref=e1128] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BibDesk
            - text: reference management software. Freeware
            - link "Qiqqa" [ref=e1129] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Qiqqa
            - text: can create an annotation report that summarizes all the annotations and notes one has made across their library of PDFs. The Text Verification Tool exports differences in documents as annotations and markups.
          - paragraph [ref=e1130]:
            - text: There are also
            - link "web annotation" [ref=e1131] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Web_annotation
            - text: systems that support annotation in pdf and other document formats. In cases where PDFs are expected to have all of the functionality of paper documents, ink annotation is required.
          - generic [ref=e1132]:
            - heading "Conversion and Information Extraction" [level=3] [ref=e1133]
            - generic [ref=e1134]:
              - text: "["
              - link "edit" [ref=e1135] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=PDF&action=edit&section=29
              - text: "]"
          - paragraph [ref=e1136]:
            - text: PDF's emphasis on preserving the visual appearance of documents across different software and hardware platforms poses challenges to the conversion of PDF documents to other
            - link "file formats" [ref=e1137] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File_format
            - text: and the targeted
            - link "extraction of information" [ref=e1138] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Information_extraction
            - text: ", such as text, images, tables,"
            - link "bibliographic information" [ref=e1139] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bibliography
            - text: ", and document"
            - link "metadata" [ref=e1140] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metadata
            - text: . Numerous tools and source code libraries support these tasks. Several labeled
            - link "datasets" [ref=e1141] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dataset
            - text: to test PDF conversion and information extraction tools exist and have been used for benchmark evaluations of the tool's performance.
            - superscript [ref=e1142]:
              - link "[94]" [ref=e1143] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-94"
          - generic [ref=e1144]:
            - heading "Alternatives" [level=2] [ref=e1145]
            - generic [ref=e1146]:
              - text: "["
              - link "edit" [ref=e1147] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=PDF&action=edit&section=30
              - text: "]"
          - note [ref=e1148]:
            - text: "Main article:"
            - link "Open XML Paper Specification § Comparison with PDF" [ref=e1149] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Open_XML_Paper_Specification#Comparison_with_PDF
          - note [ref=e1150]:
            - text: "See also:"
            - link "EPUB" [ref=e1151] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/EPUB
          - paragraph [ref=e1152]:
            - text: The
            - link "Open XML Paper Specification" [ref=e1153] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Open_XML_Paper_Specification
            - text: is a competing format used both as a page description language and as the native print spooler format for Microsoft Windows since
            - link "Windows Vista" [ref=e1154] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Windows_Vista
            - text: .
          - paragraph [ref=e1155]:
            - 'link "Mixed Object: Document Content Architecture" [ref=e1156] [cursor=pointer]':
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MODCA
            - text: is a competing format. MO:DCA-P is a part of
            - link "Advanced Function Presentation" [ref=e1157] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Advanced_Function_Presentation
            - text: .
          - generic [ref=e1158]:
            - heading "See also" [level=2] [ref=e1159]
            - generic [ref=e1160]:
              - text: "["
              - link "edit" [ref=e1161] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=PDF&action=edit&section=31
              - text: "]"
          - list [ref=e1162]:

            - listitem [ref=e1165]:

          - list [ref=e1179]:
            - listitem [ref=e1180]:
              - generic [ref=e1181]:
                - text: ^
                - 'link "Jump up to: a" [ref=e1182] [cursor=pointer]':
                  - /url: "#cite_ref-rfc8118_1-0"
                  - generic [ref=e1183]: "Jump up to:"
                  - superscript [ref=e1184]:
                    - generic [ref=e1185]: a
                - link "b" [ref=e1186] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#cite_ref-rfc8118_1-1"
                  - superscript [ref=e1187]:
                    - generic [ref=e1188]: b
              - generic [ref=e1189]:
                - generic [ref=e1190]:
                  - text: Hardy, M.; Masinter, L.; Markovic, D.; Johnson, D.; Bailey, M. (March 2017).
                  - link "The application/pdf Media Type" [ref=e1191] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8118
                  - text: .
                  - link "IETF" [ref=e1192] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Internet_Engineering_Task_Force
                  - text: .
                  - link "doi" [ref=e1193] [cursor=pointer]:

                  - text: ":"
                  - generic "Freely accessible" [ref=e1194]:

            - listitem [ref=e1198]:
              - generic [ref=e1199]:
                - text: ^
                - 'link "Jump up to: a" [ref=e1200] [cursor=pointer]':
                  - /url: "#cite_ref-pdf-ref-1.7_2-0"
                  - generic [ref=e1201]: "Jump up to:"
                  - superscript [ref=e1202]:
                    - generic [ref=e1203]: a
                - link "b" [ref=e1204] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#cite_ref-pdf-ref-1.7_2-1"
                  - superscript [ref=e1205]:
                    - generic [ref=e1206]: b
                - link "c" [ref=e1207] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#cite_ref-pdf-ref-1.7_2-2"
                  - superscript [ref=e1208]:
                    - generic [ref=e1209]: c
                - link "d" [ref=e1210] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#cite_ref-pdf-ref-1.7_2-3"
                  - superscript [ref=e1211]:
                    - generic [ref=e1212]: d
              - generic [ref=e1213]:
                - generic [ref=e1214]:
                  - text: Adobe Systems Incorporated (November 2006).
                  - link "\"PDF Reference\"" [ref=e1215] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: https://web.archive.org/web/20081001170454/https://www.adobe.com/devnet/acrobat/pdfs/pdf_reference_1-7.pdf
                  - text: (PDF). 1.7 (6th ed.). Archived from
                  - link "the original" [ref=e1216] [cursor=pointer]:

                  - text: (PDF) on October 1, 2008

                  - text: .
                - generic "ctx_ver=Z39.88-2004&rft_val_fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&rft.genre=unknown&rft.btitle=PDF+Reference&rft.series=1.7&rft.edition=6th&rft.date=2006-11&rft.au=Adobe+Systems+Incorporated&rft_id=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.adobe.com%2Fdevnet%2Facrobat%2Fpdfs%2Fpdf_reference_1-7.pdf&rfr_id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3APDF"
            - listitem [ref=e1218]:
              - link "Jump up" [ref=e1221] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#cite_ref-3"
                - text: ^
              - generic [ref=e1222]:
                - generic [ref=e1223]:
                  - text: Warnock, J. (October 14, 2004) [Original date 5 May 1995].
                  - link "\"The Camelot Project\"" [ref=e1224] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: https://www.pdfa.org/norm-refs/warnock_camelot.pdf

                  - link "\"Adobe admits new PDF password protection is weaker\"" [ref=e1562] [cursor=pointer]:

              - generic [ref=e2056]:
                - generic [ref=e2057]:
                  - link "\"PDF as Standard Print Job Format\"" [ref=e2058] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: https://web.archive.org/web/20091114130224/https://www.linuxfoundation.org/collaborate/workgroups/openprinting/pdf_as_standard_print_job_format

                    - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Linux_Foundation

                  - link "the original" [ref=e2060] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: http://www.linuxfoundation.org/collaborate/workgroups/openprinting/pdf_as_standard_print_job_format
                  - text: on November 14, 2009
                  - generic [ref=e2061]: . Retrieved January 12, 2023
                  - text: .
                - generic "ctx_ver=Z39.88-2004&rft_val_fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Ajournal&rft.genre=unknown&rft.jtitle=The+Linux+Foundation&rft.atitle=PDF+as+Standard+Print+Job+Format&rft.date=2009-10-23&rft_id=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.linuxfoundation.org%2Fcollaborate%2Fworkgroups%2Fopenprinting%2Fpdf_as_standard_print_job_format&rfr_id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3APDF"

          - navigation "Multi-purpose office document file formats" [ref=e2231]:
            - table [ref=e2232]:
              - rowgroup [ref=e2233]:
                - row "[show] v · t · e Multi-purpose office document file formats" [ref=e2234]:
                  - columnheader "[show] v · t · e Multi-purpose office document file formats" [ref=e2235]:
                    - button "[show]" [ref=e2236] [cursor=pointer]
                    - list [ref=e2238]:
                      - listitem [ref=e2239]:
                        - link "v" [ref=e2240] [cursor=pointer]:
                          - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Template:Office_document_file_formats
                        - text: ·
                      - listitem [ref=e2241]:
                        - link "t" [ref=e2242] [cursor=pointer]:
                          - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Template_talk:Office_document_file_formats
                        - text: ·
                      - listitem [ref=e2243]:
                        - link "e" [ref=e2244] [cursor=pointer]:
                          - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Office_document_file_formats
                    - generic [ref=e2245]:
                      - text: Multi-purpose
                      - link "office" [ref=e2246] [cursor=pointer]:
                        - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Office_suite
                      - link "document file formats" [ref=e2247] [cursor=pointer]:
                        - /url: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Document_file_format
                - row: · · · · · · · · · · ·
                - row: · · ·
                - row: ( · ) ·

    - contentinfo [ref=e2318]:
      - list [ref=e2319]:
        - listitem [ref=e2320]: This page was last edited on 30 April 2026, at 15:23 (UTC).
        - listitem [ref=e2321]:
          - text: Text is available under the
          - link "Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 4.0 License" [ref=e2322] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: /wiki/Wikipedia:Text_of_the_Creative_Commons_Attribution-ShareAlike_4.0_International_License
          - text: ; additional terms may apply. By using this site, you agree to the
          - link "Terms of Use" [ref=e2323] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: https://foundation.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:MyLanguage/Policy:Terms_of_Use
          - text: and
          - link "Privacy Policy" [ref=e2324] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: https://foundation.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:MyLanguage/Policy:Privacy_policy
          - text: . Wikipedia® is a registered trademark of the
          - link "Wikimedia Foundation, Inc." [ref=e2325] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: https://wikimediafoundation.org/
          - text: ", a non-profit organization."
      - list:
